channel.title: Combined Economist RSS Feed
channel.description: Combined feed of multiple Economist RSS sources with archive.is/o/nuunc links

item.title: Israel election poll tracker: will Binyamin Netanyahu hold on to power?
item.description: The Economist is tracking the contest for Israel’s Knesset

item.title: Donald Trump’s AI regime is opaque, unpredictable—and unsustainable
item.description: The administration now controls who gets access to the best models

item.title: Donald Trump’s hope for a new Middle East is premature
item.description: Recent attacks underscore the limits of diplomacy between Iran and America

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: South Africa is in denial about xenophobia
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, analyses the country’s shocking rise in anti-foreign sentiment

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The country with no army
item.description: A transatlantic rift is unsettling the land of fire and ice, writes Anton La Guardia, our diplomatic editor

item.title: El Boletín newsletter: An earthquake shakes Venezuela’s political reset
item.description: Our correspondent in Caracas reports on how disaster is testing an already fragile country

item.title: The Supreme Court has handed Donald Trump yet more power
item.description: Dozens of agencies—but not the Federal Reserve—are now subject to presidential whim

item.title: Turkey’s economic plan to win from the Iran war
item.description: The country is a case study in the spillovers of conflict—both positive and negative

item.title: Should every baby’s DNA be sequenced?
item.description: The genomic generation is on its way

item.title: America’s Balkan policy is all about gas
item.description: It is using its terminal in Krk to shoulder the green EU aside

item.title: How Americans see their country’s past, present and future
item.description: As America turns 250, our Economist/YouGov poll reveals an anxious and divided nation

item.title: Hong Kong, once a great place to raise and spend money, is halfway back
item.description: Tighter ties with the mainland have not always helped

item.title: The AI boom and geopolitics are rewiring Asia’s oceans
item.description: New cables between data centres are avoiding China and chokepoints

item.title: The rise of vibe lawyering
item.description: AI is emboldening people to represent themselves in court. The results are mixed

item.title: So far, so fast
item.description: Our report on gay marriage from 2014

item.title: Unfit
item.description: Our leader on Donald Trump’s response to a deadly far-right rally in Charlottesville in 2017

item.title: How will America respond to Venezuela’s deadly quakes?
item.description: The catastrophe could give the regime another excuse to put off elections

item.title: Big oil’s secretive trading arms are having an extraordinary year
item.description: Meet the corporate gamblers who never waste a good energy crisis

item.title: Why can’t India’s government build a decent website?
item.description: Clunky online experiences reveal the failures of Indian officialdom

item.title: China cracks down on rule-bending offshore investments
item.description: It wants mainland investors to bet on its own tech dreams not America’s

item.title: Donald Trump is kicking out Chinese firms and keeping their tech
item.description: China’s stranded assets are creating a new class of American green-tech investors

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The World Cup doesn’t understand America
item.description: John Prideaux, our executive editor, on why America is a spectacular place to host the football tournament

item.title: The Climate Issue newsletter: Europe can’t stand the heat
item.description: Oliver Morton, our planetary affairs editor, on why the continent is warming faster than any other—and adapting too slowly

item.title: Berlin is even worse equipped than Paris for Europe’s heatwave
item.description: Berliners gaze with dread at this weekend’s forecast of 40°C (104°F)

item.title: Is too much sleep as bad as too little?
item.description: The quest for the optimal amount of shut-eye

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Lessons from the Cuban revolution
item.description: In our bonus edition, Richard Cockett, a senior editor, explains why now is a timely moment to revisit the events that brought Fidel Castro to power

item.title: Drum Tower newsletter: Who is behind China’s latest AI breakthrough?
item.description: Corbin Duncan, our global correspondent, speaks to the Zhipu team on their technical progress, business model and the future of AI

item.title: Venezuela suffers its worst earthquake in a century
item.description: The impoverished country is struggling to cope with the damage from two nearly simultaneous shocks

item.title: Where will Europe’s heatwave be most deadly?
item.description: We analyse 854 cities to see which will be worst affected

item.title: AI models’ values are very different from most people’s
item.description: They are more secular and more liberal—unless they’re made in China

item.title: Latin America has turned Trumpy. That creates opportunities
item.description: Never before have the politics of the region shifted so quickly

item.title: University-for-all harms poor students the most
item.description: Time for sane standards in higher education

item.title: With Iran emboldened, its neighbours must put old divisions aside
item.description: With a common foe, they cannot afford petty feuds

item.title: Two state elections may break Malaysia’s ruling coalition
item.description: Anwar Ibrahim’s long-sought tenure as prime minister may be cut short

item.title: Asian governments are making children care for their parents
item.description: As populations age, the elderly are being neglected

item.title: Bangladesh’s main industry is battered by blackouts and rising costs
item.description: A crisis may loom

item.title: Students are doing worse than you think
item.description: Some at college or university are testing no better than ten-year-olds

item.title: J.D. Vance is heir to a more radical politics than Trumpism
item.description: …if, of course, he believes what he’s saying

item.title: America’s pro-Israel lobby considers next steps
item.description: Israel’s defenders are flailing at the White House and at the ballot box

item.title: Democrats and Republicans agree: AI is scary
item.description: It’s about the only thing that unites them

item.title: Business
Politics
The EU is just too damn slow
item.description: Why Brussels needs to pick up the pace

item.title: France’s south is testing out hard-right rule
item.description: The National Rally is mixing raw nativism with promises of fiscal liberalism

item.title: Why Germany’s cities are going broke
item.description: Bad news for residents, good news for the far right’s electoral chances

item.title: Peter Magyar is trying to fire up Hungarian democracy
item.description: A constitutional amendment would burn away the remnants of Viktor Orban’s regime

item.title: Teaching AI how people work is fraught with problems
item.description: Tacit knowledge is vital to many jobs

item.title: Why easyJet may be heading for a break-up
item.description: Its private-equity suitor may be looking to sell it for parts

item.title: UPS is losing ground to FedEx
item.description: The two logistics giants have similar strategies—and face a common threat

item.title: Medieval-style fortifications are back in the Sahel
item.description: Newly walled towns are a sign of shrivelling state authority

item.title: Does Gadi Eisenkot offer Israel a new chance?
item.description: Israelis think he may be a genuine alternative to Binyamin Netanyahu

item.title: Ebola has put Africa’s fragile health systems in the spotlight
item.description: Emergencies and aid cuts are accelerating a push for self-reliance

item.title: What a surge of anti-migrant protests says about South Africa
item.description: It is too easy for xenophobic vigilantes to take advantage of poverty and a weak state

item.title: America is using force to drive gangsters from Venezuela’s gold
item.description: It won’t be a pushover

item.title: Two powerful earthquakes hit Venezuela
item.description: Such tightly sequenced quakes are rare

item.title: The dramatic Trumpification of Latin America
item.description: Real problems, copycattery and a network of hacks have helped Donald Trump’s pals win a string of elections

item.title: Cartoon: Russia’s war fatigue grows
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: The US in Brief: The Sharpie cap stays on
item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.title: Global imbalances have little to do with Europe’s industrial woes
item.description: The EU has forgotten that it, like China, is a surplus economy

item.title: Will AI lower interest rates?
item.description: Kevin Warsh is drawing lessons about tech from Alan Greenspan—but selectively

item.title: Both Donald Trump and Giorgia Meloni are begging for trouble
item.description: The dalliance between the populist-right leaders of America and Italy is off—or is it?

item.title: Andy Burnham promises hope. Britain needs more than that
item.description: He shows little sign that he grasps the scale or urgency of the tasks that await him

item.title: The AI backlash is only getting started
item.description: Here is how to deal with it

item.title: The Gulf’s three post-war challenges
item.description: Geography, cash and confidence will decide who recovers, and who drifts

item.title: Strange new EV-makers keep appearing in China
item.description: If you can make a robo-vacuum, you can make a car

item.title: Silicon Valley has much to learn from the spreadsheet jockeys it despises
item.description: Even if financial markets disagree

item.title: Global warming has made Europe’s heatwave 2-4°C worse
item.description: The continent is warming faster than any other

item.title: Electronics can now be printed onto living tissues
item.description: From cow femurs to replacement hips and even living leaves

item.title: Do high-tech “add-ons” increase the chance that IVF will work?
item.description: A big new review suggests most such procedures don’t help

item.title: Middle East Dispatch newsletter: Syria’s honeymoon is over
item.description: An arrest has damaged the government’s image, writes Gareth Browne, our Middle East correspondent

item.title: Why big AI labs are hiring so many philosophers
item.description: The technology presents all sorts of thorny problems—a philosopher’s favourite kind

item.title: The Supreme Court is limiting plaintiffs’ ability to hold the powerful to account
item.description: Two rulings mark a subtle but important shift

item.title: America’s data-centre backlash puts the AI boom at risk
item.description: Opposition is spreading across the country

item.title: Waiving sanctions on Iranian oil is a huge concession by America
item.description: It will enrich an adversary

item.title: Why macro trading is hard
item.description: More difficult than knowing what to buy is how much

item.title: Russia’s Crimean conquest is turning into a deadly mess
item.description: Ukrainian drones have made the resort region a battlezone

item.title: Child care is becoming more affordable
item.description: Our ranking compares nursery bills to income in 36 rich countries

item.title: Smartphones and AI are remaking rural India
item.description: Villages have fallen in love with short-form videos and chatbots

item.title: El Boletín newsletter: What Colombia’s rightward swing says about the country
item.description: Claire McQue, a Latin America contributor, reports from Bogotá on why voters embraced a tough-talking political outsider

item.title: Russia’s war economy has problems—but is not about to crash
item.description: Vladimir Putin is still able to fund his aggression

item.title: Looking for a winner from the Iran war?
item.description: California’s pistachio farmers have gained, for now

item.title: How to turn compute into a financial asset
item.description: Entrepreneurs, exchange operators and AI firms are creating tradable instruments backed by processing power

item.title: China can be a useful bogeyman in American politics
item.description: Accusations of influence-peddling can muddy what is really going on

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: America’s AI power grab
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: The Trump-loving right wins Colombia’s presidency
item.description: Abelardo de la Espriella promises an iron fist and economic miracles

item.title: American police killings are rising, even as murder rates fall
item.description: More aggressive cops are less restrained

item.title: Zombie unicorns are haunting Silicon Valley
item.description: Years of frothy valuations have created a nightmare

item.title: Will Israel undermine America’s peace with Iran?
item.description: Doing so would put it on a collision course with its most important ally

item.title: China is having another AI moment
item.description: A new model has narrowed the gap with America

item.title: The unlikely city welcoming Delhi’s intellectual refugees
item.description: The political climate in India’s capital is stifling free thought

item.title: How stretched is the American consumer?
item.description: The household saving rate has plunged—but don’t panic

item.title: How good is infant formula?
item.description: Perhaps less than its marketers say

item.title: The deceptive rise of the “short king”
item.description: Small men have won over social media, but continue to struggle in the dating market

item.title: The G7 has nudged open a window for diplomacy in Ukraine
item.description: Europeans see a hopeful shift in America’s attitude

item.title: Anthropic’s astonishing commercial success makes it a target
item.description: The cultlike firm is battling with the government for control of superpowered AI

item.title: Don’t restrict Chinese biotech
item.description: Patients benefit from faster, cheaper treatments, wherever they are invented

item.title: Britain is not ready to rejoin the EU
item.description: After a lost decade, it is time to focus on the future

item.title: AI has granted America vast new power
item.description: Its government is now the gatekeeper to frontier models—and most compute

item.title: From Philippine staple to global sensation: the rise of ube
item.description: The country is struggling to keep up with soaring demand

item.title: Taiwan’s opposition leader faced a tough crowd in America
item.description: Many still have doubts about her outreach to Xi Jinping

item.title: Myanmar’s junta revives a hated dam to court China
item.description: A dam halted by protests in 2011 is back, but not for the energy it will produce

item.title: The real winner in Myanmar’s civil war is China
item.description: It doesn’t want to fix the war-torn country but is fine managing it

item.title: News extortion is rife in China
item.description: The country’s censors may be making it worse

item.title: How China still outworks the West
item.description: A medal scheme masks the identity of its toughest labourers

item.title: Meet the world’s new peacemakers
item.description: Autocracies are taking over conflict mediation. The deals they are making look different

item.title: The left is coming for Democratic incumbents
item.description: In primary challenges, Mayor Zohran Mamdani is trying to install allies in Congress

item.title: Pet-custody laws in America are changing
item.description: More states are weighing pets’ interests, not just their price

item.title: Populist president, meet socialist mayor
item.description: America’s capital is poised to elect a new leftist leader

item.title: Cartoon: Donald Trump’s Iran deal
Business
Politics
How plants keep tabs on the competition
item.description: They grow faster when their rivals are doing the same

item.title: Europeans should learn to love the air-conditioner
item.description: Green electricity means never having to say sorry for lowering the thermostat

item.title: Britain’s departure made Europe more French
item.description: A smaller EU has learned to live without Britain

item.title: How to launch a tech product
item.description: Create a slick, irony-free corporate safe space

item.title: The Iran war has boosted Equinor, Norway’s energy giant
item.description: But it cannot turn a greying cash cow into a sprightly upstart

item.title: Is the staple meal in Nigeria and Ghana becoming a luxury?
item.description: What the rising prices of jollof rice show about Nigeria and Ghana’s economies

item.title: The Iran war meant an economic crisis for Africa
item.description: It was the third shock of the 2020s to hit the continent

item.title: A flimsy deal will stop the bombing and restart the oil
item.description: Whether it can do anything more will be the question that defines the next few months

item.title: The Colombian diaspora is overwhelmingly right-wing
item.description: But not in Russia

item.title: The Gen-Z streamer from Medellín influencing Colombia’s election
item.description: Algorithms, not debates, will determine the country’s next president

item.title: War has strengthened the Islamic Republic. Peace could split it
item.description: For now, the hardliners are in the ascendant

item.title: A new golden age for Japanese banks comes with a catch
item.description: Smaller lenders are saddled with low-return bonds they cannot sell

item.title: Europe buys the future, America builds it
item.description: Does that matter? A high-stakes transatlantic spat has erupted over GDP figures

item.title: Tata’s big bets are yet to pay off
item.description: The Indian conglomerate’s boardroom drama highlights a speculative strategy

item.title: Republicans are desperate to move on from the Iran war
item.description: The conflict will have stubborn political after-effects

item.title: India’s new economy still faces an old problem
item.description: Family-run conglomerates make the stock market a tricky place to invest

item.title: Donald Trump gambles that Iran wants money more than power
item.description: The peace deal is all carrot and no stick

item.title: As American cities grapple with homelessness, one offers a fix
item.description: Denver’s answer to a stubborn problem

item.title: Tournament of losers
item.description: The World Cup is a festival for corporate has-beens

item.title: Germany’s left-wing Die Linke party has won over the young
item.description: The populists promise a “scorching hot socialist summer” of protest

item.title: Iran’s battered economy will take years to recover from the war
item.description: Its leaders want sanctions relief and aid for reconstruction

item.title: Ancient DNA is rewriting the history of plague
item.description: Dense cities do not seem to have been necessary for outbreaks of the disease

item.title: The chocolate industry is built on the labour of bloodsucking midges
item.description: Researchers have now confirmed how cacao plants are pollinated

item.title: Fox, Roku and the next phase of the streaming wars
item.description: A $22bn deal creates a formidable new power in Hollywood

item.title: Introducing our Business in Brief newsletter
item.description: Every day we will deliver concise analysis of the stories driving business and markets worldwide

item.title: Albania’s flamingo protests target Donald Trump’s son-in-law
item.description: Jared Kushner’s beach development brings trouble for a prime minister

item.title: Deal or no deal, oil prices will stay volatile for months
item.description: The pre-war days of $60 crude are not coming back soon

item.title: Asian allies are doomed to hug Donald Trump close
item.description: Forget middle-power coalitions. Japan and its neighbours know that only America can deter China

item.title: America’s bull market has entered its manic phase
item.description: Options markets show optimism is giving way to euphoria

item.title: The coming El Niño could be the strongest ever recorded
item.description: That is bad news for a warming world

item.title: Scammers are preying on America’s illegal immigrants
item.description: Fake lawyers see opportunity in Donald Trump’s crackdown

item.title: Did AI write this article?
item.description: Perish the thought. But we tracked how artificial intelligence is changing five creative fields

item.title: Travel Brazil’s mirror-state to see the country’s future
item.description: No one has ever been elected president in Brazil without carrying Minas Gerais

item.title: The end of the war in Iran threatens “glorious failure” for Israel
item.description: Donald Trump’s deal with Iran leaves America’s ally without any strategic gains

item.title: Meet the world’s top AI-pilled economists
item.description: Most of them are not found in ivory towers

item.title: America’s carmakers cannot escape Chinese EVs for ever
item.description: Can they stay relevant?

item.title: The War Room newsletter: What eight years as defence editor looks like
item.description: From flying fighter jets to travelling on destroyers, Shashank Joshi pens his final edition of this newsletter

item.title: Comfort meets constraint in China’s most “liveable” city
item.description: A high-income, low-pressure existence is not all it’s cracked up to be

item.title: The terrifying new air war in Ukraine
item.description: Russia is dominating the missile war by default, but Ukraine hopes that will change

item.title: A deal is only the beginning of the end of the US-Iran war
item.description: And they could start fighting again in the hope of getting a better one

item.title: Donald Trump’s blocking of Anthropic is capricious and chaotic
item.description: America’s closest allies are shellshocked

item.title: Political manoeuvres are delaying a spy law and new intelligence chief
item.description: Crucial surveillance authorities have lapsed

item.title: The problem with Narendra Modi’s airport-building frenzy
item.description: Build it and they will come. But how will they leave?

item.title: The Californication of middle-class Chinese diets
item.description: China wants more healthy, clean and even foreign food

item.title: Companies are scrambling to curtail soaring AI costs
item.description: For the heaviest users, tokenmaxxing is losing its appeal

item.title: Does Donald Trump make Latin America a good bet?
item.description: Nowhere in the developing world has done so well out of the past year

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Can Tocqueville explain America today?
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, takes a road trip to mark the country’s 250th birthday

item.title: The Swiss would be foolish to cap their population at 10m
item.description: Immigration sometimes needs brakes, but the proposal would be like driving into a wall

item.title: A new drug targets one of cancer’s master switches
item.description: The pancreatic-cancer drug could be the first of an entirely new class of treatments

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: A World Cup paradox
item.description: How we chose this week’s image

item.title: The value of SpaceX rockets on its stock-market debut
item.description: Elon Musk has become the world’s first trillionaire

item.title: The best way to celebrate America at 250 is to get behind the wheel
item.description: Preferably with our new podcast series for company

item.title: Japan is rethinking its divorce laws
item.description: A move to introduce joint custody marks a big shift

item.title: In China ride-hailing work is a last resort for rural labourers
item.description: But now Shenzhen says it has too many of them

item.title: China’s notorious university-entrance exam is changing
item.description: Demography and AI are set to transform the gaokao

item.title: On America’s 250th, go back to “Democracy in America”
item.description: In praise of Alexis de Tocqueville

item.title: Social media is behind both “teen takeovers” and the outrage they fuel
item.description: Cities struggle to respond to big gatherings of young folk

item.title: America’s quintessential places are getting old, fast
item.description: Welcome to the greying suburbs

item.title: Cartoon: The search for peace in Iran
Business
Politics
Ukraine is transplanting its industrial heart to the west
item.description: Soviet-built factory towns are in the wrong spot now that Russia is the enemy

item.title: Another new boss aims to fix the world’s biggest chocolate-maker
item.description: Will Hein Schumacher’s turnaround plan for Barry Callebaut work?

item.title: Too many people are shockingly bad at prioritisation
item.description: Choosing where to focus is among the most important skills

item.title: The world’s wealthy are migrating like never before
item.description: A booming industry of advisers is easing their passage

item.title: Fighting in Mogadishu risks making a weak state weaker
item.description: A presidential power grab has convulsed Somali politics

item.title: Could Eritrea come in from the cold?
item.description: Isaias Afwerki, its reclusive dictator, has spotted an opening to reconcile with America

item.title: The first-ever robotic rescue at sea is a milestone
item.description: An American drone recovers downed pilots in the Strait of Hormuz

item.title: Iran has lost its fear of war
item.description: A once-cautious regime is making a risky bet on low-level conflict

item.title: Techno-libertarians are flocking to the Caribbean
item.description: Angry locals are trying to shut them down

item.title: Can India’s cockroach party become a political movement?
item.description: The government is betting that the answer is no

item.title: Asian activists say too much egg production is cruel
item.description: They are hatching ingenious schemes

item.title: How big are China’s emerging industries?
item.description: Probably not big enough to offset the drag from the old

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The year America reckoned with AIDS
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, on how activists forced the country to confront the crisis

item.title: For its own sake, China should change its growth model
item.description: It is suffering economic costs for its industrial dominance

item.title: A trade war between the EU and China seems inevitable
item.description: Europe sees Chinese subsidies, China sees European weakness

item.title: Stears wants to be Africa’s Bloomberg terminal
item.description: As the continent’s markets mature, high-quality financial analysis is more essential than ever

item.title: The world’s strategic oil reserves are running out fast
item.description: They cannot hold back the energy crunch for ever

item.title: How to share AI riches
item.description: From Donald Trump to Sam Altman, the idea of redistributing them is catching on. Does it make sense?

item.title: Ukraine’s war is now longer than the first world war
item.description: The ghost of Versailles haunts Ukraine’s peace

item.title: The Knicks represent New York—and capitalism—at its best
item.description: That is why New Yorkers love them

item.title: Forget the World Cup. Culture is becoming more fragmented
item.description: Farewell to the monoculture

item.title: Syria is an unexpected beneficiary of the Gulf war
item.description: The revival of an old oil-export route from Iraq to the Mediterranean helps Syria’s new regime

item.title: How to win the World Cup
item.description: Being rich helps, but being open to immigration works best of all

item.title: American capitalism is run by millionaires, not billionaires
item.description: They hide in plain sight—and wield enormous power

item.title: New techniques can predict and prevent lung cancer
item.description: A molecular signature can identify those most at risk

item.title: The World Cup has always been beset by scandal and strife
item.description: So has FIFA, the outfit that administers it

item.title: The World Cup paradox
item.description: How the rules of both entertainment and soft power are being rewritten

item.title: Too much Chinese science is ignored by the West
item.description: A bad reputation and cultural ignorance are probably responsible

item.title: Donald Trump’s least bad option in Iran
item.description: He must swallow his pride and accept a deal worse than the pre-war status quo

item.title: America’s mayors join the scrabble to become influencers
item.description: A dispatch from Long Beach, California

item.title: Fear of the SaaSpocalypse is tormenting techland
item.description: Software once ate the world. Now it is in danger of eating itself

item.title: An interview with South Korea’s president
item.description: Lee Jae Myung has put his country on track again, but challenges loom

item.title: Wall Street’s undignified SpaceX mania
item.description: Is that a rocket in your lobby, or are you just happy to see me?

item.title: Why Turkey likes NATO again
item.description: The alliance is in trouble, but Erdogan is enthusiastic

item.title: Ukrainian strikes are inflicting pain deep inside Russia
item.description: Our modelling suggests they are doing even more damage than commonly assumed

item.title: Why strongmen are wrong to loathe Europe
item.description: The leaders of America, China and Russia scorn consensual politics. That is a mistake

item.title: What happens when a presidential vote is a drawn-out contest?
item.description: Peru, looking for its ninth president in a decade, is in a tight spot

item.title: The Federal Reserve must soon give Donald Trump bad news
item.description: Kevin Warsh, the unlucky new chairman, has seen his case for lower interest rates disintegrate

item.title: Apple’s new Siri is a dark horse in the AI race
item.description: The iPhone-maker does not need to build models to cash in on the technology

item.title: Armenia’s election is a setback for Vladimir Putin
item.description: Russia’s dirty tricks are failing to stop its pivot to the West

item.title: China is innovative. Its economy is a mess. Which matters more?
item.description: A question that will define the 21st century

item.title: A bidding war erupts for the world’s oldest bank
item.description: Monte dei Paschi di Siena is suddenly all the rage

item.title: How Israel is frustrating Donald Trump’s Iran plans
item.description: Binyamin Netanyahu has defied America twice in recent days

item.title: The ageing protesters trying to topple Washington’s “ego arch”
item.description: Donald Trump has energised his fellow boomers in opposition

item.title: The War Room newsletter: When war becomes a political aesthetic
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, examines why conflict exerts a “strange pull”

item.title: A dropout-turned-influencer shakes up Chinese science
item.description: Fraud is rampant—but his videos mean everyone is now talking about it

item.title: How artificial intelligence got better at building itself
item.description: What does “recursive self-improvement” mean for the technology?

item.title: Nukes were off the agenda as Xi Jinping visited North Korea
item.description: China is wary of Russian influence and another Trump-Kim summit

item.title: The World Cup will test Mexico’s control over its territory
item.description: Donald Trump, who says the government is too weak to combat the drug gangs, will be watching

item.title: Money troubles are driving India’s states to drink
item.description: Things are so dire that one state has produced a rational alcohol policy

item.title: Robots could soon be delivering your pizza
item.description: An Estonian startup says its machines are now cheaper than human couriers

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: A modest proposal on Cuba
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, on why the island should become America’s 51st state

item.title: How hot is America’s labour market?
item.description: At the moment, balmy. But it wouldn’t take a lot to require some Fed air-conditioning

item.title: Should priests have to report child abuse disclosed in confession?
item.description: American states take different approaches

item.title: The chemicals that reduce wrinkles
item.description: Vitamins, applied properly, can partially reverse the effects of ageing

item.title: America’s secretary of war pulls his punches on China
item.description: The Pentagon’s strategy for Asia contains a very big hole

item.title: Worries about migrants imperil South Korea’s shipbuilding boom
item.description: Compared with most rich countries, the foreign workforce is still small

item.title: Sex tourists fuel outrage about vice in Japan
item.description: The debates do not have women’s interests at heart

item.title: The rise of One Nation is melting Australian politics
item.description: Could the tumult produce a new party?

item.title: Ma Ning will proudly represent China at the World Cup
item.description: The referee is succeeding where the men’s national team is not

item.title: Xi Jinping gives China’s crack scientists new jobs inside government
item.description: Tech rivalry is reshaping the ruling elite

item.description: Startups are combining AI and genetics to make more food for less money

item.description: The region’s rulers want to reduce their dependence on Western arms

item.description: Unable to control external threats, they are squeezing their own people

item.description: To help their cause, they are adopting tropes and rhetoric from the American right

item.title: India’s population will soon be falling—probably quite fast
item.description: Neither widespread poverty, nor high rates of marriage nor relatively young mothers are sustaining fertility

item.title: Do houseplants improve air quality?
item.description: Air purifiers are more effective and easier to maintain

item.description: The threat from new pathogens is an even graver danger than AI-backed hackers
